### RSC Advances

## Recent trends and future perspectives of thermoelectric materials and their applications

![Check for updates](https://crossmark-cdn.crossref.org/widget/v2.0/logos/CROSSMARK_Color_horizontal.svg)
![ORCID logo](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)

\* 

Corresponding authors

a

Centre for Molecular and Nanomedical Sciences, International Research Centre, Sathyabama Institute of Science and Technology (Deemed to be University), Chennai 600 119, Tamilnadu, India
  
**E-mail:**
[mrajasekar\_83@yahoo.com](mailto:mrajasekar_83@yahoo.com "mrajasekar_83@yahoo.com"), [drmrajasekar.irc@sathyabama.ac.in](mailto:drmrajasekar.irc@sathyabama.ac.in "drmrajasekar.irc@sathyabama.ac.in")   
**Tel:** +91-9710230530

### Abstract

This review explores the ever-evolving landscape of thermoelectric materials, focusing on the latest trends and innovations in ceramics, thermally conductive gel-like materials, metals, nanoparticles, polymers, and silicon. Thermoelectric materials have garnered significant attention for their capability to convert waste heat into electrical power, positioning them as promising candidates for energy harvesting and cooling applications. This review distinguishes itself by highlighting recent advancements in synthesis methods, advanced doping strategies, and nanostructuring techniques that have markedly enhanced material performance. It provides a comprehensive analysis of the controlled properties concerning their synthesis parameters, such as electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity. Furthermore, this work delves into the emerging applications of thermoelectric devices across diverse fields, including automotive, aerospace, wearable electronics, and industrial waste heat recovery. By offering forward-looking insights, this review outlines thermoelectric devices' challenges and future prospects, underscoring their potential to contribute to sustainable energy solutions and efficient thermal management systems. By integrating current trends with future projections, this review offers a timely and comprehensive roadmap for researchers and engineers dedicated to advancing next-generation thermoelectric technologies.

## Latest Advancements in Thermoelectric Materials for Waste Heat Recovery

Manufacturing facilities across the globe release approximately 70% of their energy input as waste heat. This untapped thermal energy represents both a significant inefficiency and an opportunity for innovative recovery solutions. Thermoelectric materials, which convert temperature differentials directly into electrical power, offer manufacturing professionals a sophisticated approach to capturing and repurposing this otherwise lost energy resource.

## Principles of Thermoelectric Materials

At the heart of thermoelectric waste heat recovery lie the Seebeck and Peltier effects. When two dissimilar conductors or semiconductors form a closed circuit with junctions at different temperatures, an electric current flows - this is the Seebeck effect. Conversely, when a current is applied, heat is absorbed at one junction and released at the other - the Peltier effect. Thermoelectric materials leverage these phenomena to generate electricity from thermal gradients.

The efficiency of a thermoelectric material depends on its figure of merit (ZT), which factors in electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and the Seebeck coefficient. Higher ZT values indicate better performance. Modern materials like bismuth telluride (Bi2Te3) and lead telluride (PbTe) boast ZT values around 1, enabling conversion efficiencies up to 5-8% under typical industrial conditions.

## Advancements in Thermoelectric Materials

Recent breakthroughs have propelled thermoelectric waste heat recovery to new heights:

**High-Performance Alloys**: The development of advanced alloys and composites, such as skutterudites and half-Heuslers, has significantly enhanced the efficiency of thermoelectric generators (TEGs). These materials represent a paradigm shift, broadening TEG applications in waste heat recovery.

**Nanostructured Materials**: Researchers have ventured into nanostructured thermoelectric materials, which offer unique properties that amplify performance. Nanoscale features like quantum dots and superlattices suppress thermal conductivity while maintaining electrical conductivity, boosting ZT values.

**Silicon Nanowires**: Silicon nanowires have shown promising results for high-temperature waste heat conversion. Measurements reveal much higher efficiency than bulk silicon and other nanostructured silicon-based thermoelectrics, particularly at elevated temperatures around 427°C (801°F).

## Thermoelectric Generators in Industrial Applications

Thermoelectric devices find use in various industrial waste heat recovery applications:

**Automotive Exhaust**: TEGs can be integrated into vehicle exhaust systems to convert waste heat into electrical power, improving fuel efficiency and reducing emissions. BMW, Ford, and other manufacturers have explored this technology.

**Industrial Processes**: Metal and glass processing facilities generate substantial waste heat. Thermoelectric modules can be installed on furnaces, kilns, and other equipment to recover this thermal energy and offset electricity consumption.

**Curved Devices**: Innovative curved or circular thermoelectric legs have been developed to conform to hot surfaces like exhaust pipes. Gentherm has demonstrated circular TEGs for more effective vehicle exhaust heat recovery.

## Environmental Benefits and Sustainability

Thermoelectric waste heat recovery aligns with the principles of sustainable manufacturing:

**Life Cycle Assessment**: Studies show that thermoelectric modules exhibit significant positive environmental benefits over their life cycle, comparable to those from solar and wind energy. Despite production-related impacts, the ecological advantages of TEGs are substantial.

**Eco-Friendly Materials**: Recent research has focused on developing eco-friendly, non-toxic, and earth-abundant thermoelectric materials. Sulfides, tetrahedrites, and silicides offer sustainable alternatives to traditional telluride-based compounds.

**Circular Economy**: Adopting a circular economy approach can minimize the environmental impacts of thermoelectric module production. Addressing major negative contributors and implementing end-of-life recycling strategies enhances the sustainability of TEG waste heat recovery systems.

## Implementing Thermoelectric Waste Heat Recovery

Integrating thermoelectric generators into manufacturing processes requires careful planning and optimization:

**Heat Source Characterization**: Identifying the temperature, flux, and accessibility of waste heat sources is crucial. Process engineers must assess the thermal profile of exhaust streams, furnace walls, and other potential recovery points to determine the feasibility of TEG installation.

**Module Design**: Thermoelectric modules must be designed to match the heat source characteristics and optimize power output. Factors like leg geometry, material selection, and thermal interface management play critical roles in TEG performance. Simulation tools and prototyping help refine the module design for specific applications.

**System Integration**: Incorporating TEGs into existing manufacturing infrastructure presents challenges. Space constraints, mounting requirements, and electrical interconnections must be addressed. Collaborative efforts between thermoelectric experts and plant engineers ensure seamless integration and minimal disruption to production processes.

**Power Conditioning**: The electrical output from thermoelectric modules requires conditioning for practical use. Maximum power point tracking (MPPT) and voltage regulation circuits optimize the power extracted from TEGs. Energy storage systems like batteries or supercapacitors can buffer the generated electricity for later consumption.

## Overcoming Challenges and Future Prospects

While thermoelectric waste heat recovery holds immense potential, certain challenges must be addressed:

**Material Optimization**: Enhancing the figure of merit (ZT) of thermoelectric materials remains an ongoing research focus. Nanostructuring techniques, band structure engineering, and novel compositions are being explored to push the efficiency limits of TEGs.

**Scalability and Cost**: Transitioning from laboratory-scale devices to industrial-scale systems requires manufacturing process optimization. Reducing material and fabrication costs through economies of scale and advanced production methods will make TEGs more economically viable for widespread adoption.

**Durability and Reliability**: Thermoelectric modules must withstand harsh industrial environments, including high temperatures, thermal cycling, and mechanical stresses. Advances in materials science and packaging technologies are improving the long-term stability and reliability of TEGs.

**System-Level Optimization**: Integrating thermoelectric waste heat recovery into the broader energy management strategy of manufacturing facilities is essential. Optimizing the interplay between TEGs, heat exchangers, and other energy systems maximizes the overall efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the solution.

As sustainable manufacturing practices gain momentum, thermoelectric waste heat recovery is poised to play a pivotal role. The Sustainable Manufacturing Expo serves as a platform for industry leaders, researchers, and technology providers to collaborate and drive the adoption of this transformative technology.

By showcasing the latest advancements in thermoelectric materials, module design, and system integration, the Expo fosters knowledge sharing and networking opportunities. Attendees can engage with experts, learn about real-world case studies, and explore partnerships to implement thermoelectric waste heat recovery in their own facilities.

Moreover, the Expo highlights the environmental and economic benefits of thermoelectric technology. Recovering waste heat not only reduces energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions but also provides a reliable source of electricity that can offset utility costs. As carbon pricing and sustainability regulations intensify, investing in thermoelectric waste heat recovery becomes an increasingly attractive proposition for forward-thinking manufacturers.

The future of sustainable manufacturing is intertwined with the development and deployment of innovative technologies like thermoelectric waste heat recovery. By embracing this opportunity and participating in events like the Sustainable Manufacturing Expo, industry professionals can position themselves at the forefront of the green manufacturing revolution.

Don't miss the chance to explore the potential of thermoelectric materials for waste heat recovery in your own operations. Register today for the Sustainable Manufacturing Expo and take a proactive step towards a more sustainable and efficient future for your organization.

## The Future of Sustainable Manufacturing

As the manufacturing industry continues to evolve, thermoelectric waste heat recovery stands at the forefront of sustainable practices. By harnessing the untapped potential of thermal energy, manufacturers can significantly reduce their carbon footprint, improve energy efficiency, and drive long-term cost savings. The advancements in thermoelectric materials, from high-performance alloys to nanostructured compounds, are opening new doors for industrial applications.

However, the journey towards widespread adoption of thermoelectric waste heat recovery is not without challenges. Continued research and development efforts are crucial to optimize material properties, enhance scalability, and improve the economic viability of thermoelectric generators. Collaboration between industry leaders, researchers, and technology providers is essential to overcome these hurdles and accelerate the deployment of sustainable solutions.

As a manufacturing professional, you have the power to shape the future of your organization and the industry as a whole. By staying informed about the latest advancements in thermoelectric materials and exploring their potential for your operations, you can position yourself as a sustainability leader. Embracing innovative technologies like thermoelectric waste heat recovery not only benefits the environment but also unlocks new opportunities for growth and competitiveness.

## Discover the Potential of Thermoelectric Waste Heat Recovery at the Sustainable Manufacturing Expo

The Sustainable Manufacturing Expo is your gateway to the world of thermoelectric waste heat recovery and other cutting-edge sustainable technologies. This premier event brings together industry experts, innovators, and decision-makers to showcase the latest advancements and share valuable insights. By attending the Expo, you'll have the opportunity to engage with thermoelectric material suppliers, module manufacturers, and system integrators, gaining firsthand knowledge of how this technology can be applied to your specific manufacturing processes. Don't miss this chance to explore the potential of thermoelectric waste heat recovery and take a proactive step towards a more sustainable and efficient future for your organization. [Register today](https://xpressreg.net/register/smex0225/start.asp) and be part of the green manufacturing revolution at the Sustainable Manufacturing Expo.
